questions.question: What does the transition function δ of a deterministic TM map?
options: Q × Γ → Q × Γ × {L, R}
Q × Σ → Q × Σ
Q → Γ × {L, R}
questions.explanation: For a deterministic TM, δ takes a state and a tape symbol and returns a new state, a symbol to write, and a head direction.

questions.question: Which of the following is TRUE about multitape TMs vs single-tape TMs?
options: Multitape TMs can compute strictly more functions
Any multitape TM can be simulated by a single-tape TM
Single-tape TMs are more powerful than multitape TMs
They recognize different classes of languages
questions.explanation: A single-tape TM can simulate k tapes using encoding tricks. The simulation adds at most a quadratic time overhead but recognizes the same languages.

questions.question: What class of languages do Turing machines recognize?
options: Context-free languages
Regular languages
Recursively enumerable languages
Context-sensitive languages only
questions.explanation: TMs recognize exactly the recursively enumerable (Turing-recognizable) languages — a superset of all other Chomsky hierarchy classes.

questions.question: What is a Universal Turing Machine?
options: A TM that accepts every possible input
A TM that simulates any other TM given its encoding and input
A TM with infinitely many states
A TM that always halts in polynomial time
questions.explanation: A UTM takes ⟨M, w⟩ as input and simulates TM M on input w. It is the theoretical basis for programmable computers.

questions.question: What makes a Turing machine more powerful than a pushdown automaton?
options: It can read input faster
It has a read-write tape that can be traversed in both directions with unbounded storage
It can process multiple inputs simultaneously
It uses randomization
questions.explanation: A Turing machine has an infinite read-write tape that serves as both input and working memory. Unlike a PDA's stack (which only accesses the top), the TM can read and write at any tape position and move in both directions. This enables it to recognize recursively enumerable languages.
